en.extract:
The Bornean stubtail (Urosphena whiteheadi) is a species of bird in the cettiid warbler family Cettiidae. It is endemic to the island of Borneo, where it inhabits forest floors and undergrowth in montane forests at elevations of 750–3,150 m (2,460–10,330 ft). It is a small, short-tailed warbler, measuring 9.5–10 cm (3.7–3.9 in) in length and having an average mass of 10.4 g (0.37 oz). The tops of the head and the upperparts are brown, with whitish underparts that turn grey at the sides of the breast and the flanks. The supercilium (stripe above the eye) is long and buffish-brown, with an equally long dark grey eyestripe (stripe through the eye) and a thin yellow eye-ring. Both sexes are similar.
The species feeds on invertebrates, especially green aphids, foraging in a mouse-like manner on the ground and in undergrowth. Nests are made out of reddish plant fibres, with incubation taking 24 days on average. It is classified as being of least concern by the International Union for Conservation of Nature due to its sufficiently large range and stable population.


Taxonomy and systematics
The Bornean stubtail was described as Orthnocichla whiteheadi by the British ornithologist Richard Bowdler Sharpe in 1888 on the basis of specimens from Mount Kinabalu, Borneo. It was later moved to the genus Tesia. In 1942, the American ornithologist Jean Delacour moved the species into the subgenus Urosphena within the genus Cettia. The American ornithologist Ben King raised Urosphena to the status of a genus in 1989.
The name of the genus, Urosphena, means wedge-tailed and is derived from the Ancient Greek words oura (tail) and sphēnos (wedge). The specific name whiteheadi refers to John Whitehead, a British explorer who collected the specimens based on which this species was described. Bornean stubtail is the official common name designated by the International Ornithologists' Union (IOU). Other common names for the species are short-tailed bush warbler, short-tailed stubtail, and Whitehead's stubtail.
The Bornean stubtail is classified by the IOU as one of 32 species in the cettiid warbler family Cettiidae. However, some authorities classify Cettiidae as a subfamily in an expanded Scotocercidae. Within the family, it is currently placed in Urosphena, a genus of three species of undistinguished brownish birds with short tails. A 2011 study of mitochondrial and nuclear DNA found that within the family, the present species is most closely related to the Asian and Timor stubtails. These species are sister (most closely related) to a clade (group of organisms descending from a common ancestor) formed by the pale-footed bush warbler and Neumann's warbler. The following cladogram shows relationships within the genus according to the study:


Description

The Bornean stubtail is a very small warbler with long legs and a very short tail, measuring 9.5–10 cm (3.7–3.9 in) in length and having an average mass of 10.4 g (0.37 oz). The top of the head and the upperparts are brown, while the underparts are greyish-white, turning grey at the side of the breast and the flanks. The belly is often light yellow-brown. The supercilium (stripe above the eye) is long and buffish-brown, with an equally long dark grey eyestripe (stripe through the eye) and a thin yellow eye-ring. The cheeks and ear-coverts are orange-buff. The legs are pale pink, the bill is dark brownish-black, and the iris is blackish. Both sexes look similar.


Vocalisations
The Bornean stubtail's vocalisations are poorly known, with the species usually staying silent. Its song is a single extremely high-pitched note 0.3–0.5 seconds long. The most common sound is a barely audible, high-pitched tsit-tst tseee or tzi-tzi-tzeeee 1.4 seconds long. Another vocalisation is a slightly lower, trilled piririt around 0.7 seconds long.


Distribution and habitat
Endemic to Borneo, the Bornean stubtail is found in the mountain ranges of Borneo, from Mount Kinabalu south to Liang Kubung, the Müller Mountains, Mount Dulit, and Gunung Menyapa. It has also been recorded from the southeastern part of the Meratus Mountains in the southeastern part of the island. The species inhabits the forest floor and undergrowth of montane forests at altitudes of 750–3,150 m (2,460–10,330 ft).


Behaviour and ecology
The species is usually found alone. It has a generation length of 3.6 years.
The Bornean stubtail feeds on invertebrates, especially green aphids. It forages on the ground and in dense undergrowth, creeping about in an inconspicuous manner more like that of a mouse than a bird. However, it can sometimes be quite curious and docile.
Nests consist mostly of reddish plant fibres and are built on mossy banks. The incubation of the eggs takes an average of 24 days, which is unusually long compared to other species in its range. The length is caused by the long periods of time (around 6–8 hours every day) that parents spend away from the nest.


Status
The Bornean stubtail is classified as being of least concern by the International Union for Conservation of Nature due to its sufficiently large range and stable population. Its population has not been estimated, but it is a common species above elevations of 2,000 m (6,600 ft). It is present in some protected areas like Kinabalu Park.

The '''Bornean stubtail''' ('''''Urosphena whiteheadi''''') is a [[species]] of bird in the cettiid warbler [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] [[Cettiidae]]. It is endemic to the island of [[Borneo]], where it inhabits [[forest floor]]s and [[undergrowth]] in [[montane forest]]s at elevations of {{Convert|750–3150|m|ft|abbr=on}}. It is a small, short-tailed warbler, measuring {{Convert|9.5–10|cm|in|abbr=on}} in length and having an average mass of {{Convert|10.4|g|oz|abbr=on}}. The tops of the head and the {{Birdgloss|upperparts}} are brown, with whitish {{Birdgloss|underparts}} that turn grey at the sides of the breast and the {{Birdgloss|flanks}}. The {{Birdgloss|supercilium}} (stripe above the eye) is long and [[Buff (color)|buffish]]-brown, with an equally long dark grey {{Birdgloss|eyestripe}} (stripe through the eye) and a thin yellow [[eye-ring]]. Both sexes are similar.

The species feeds on [[invertebrate]]s, especially green [[aphid]]s, foraging in a mouse-like manner on the ground and in undergrowth. Nests are made out of reddish plant fibres, with incubation taking 24 days on average. It is classified as being of [[Least-concern species|least concern]] by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]] due to its sufficiently large range and stable population.

== Taxonomy and systematics ==
The Bornean stubtail was [[Species description|described]] as ''Orthnocichla whiteheadi'' by the British [[ornithologist]] [[Richard Bowdler Sharpe]] in 1888 on the basis of specimens from [[Mount Kinabalu]], [[Borneo]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last=Sharpe |first=Richard Bowdler |date=1888 |title=Diagnoses of some new species of birds obtained on the mountain of Kina Balu by Mr. John Whitehead |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/16280230#page/506/mode/1up |journal=[[Ibis (journal)|Ibis]] |language=en |volume=30 |issue=4 |pages=478–479 |doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1888.tb08508.x |lccn=79010132 |oclc=1377260 |via=[[Biodiversity Heritage Library]]}}</ref> It was later moved to the [[genus]] ''[[Tesia]]''.<ref name="Chasen" /> In 1942, the American ornithologist [[Jean Delacour]] moved the species into the [[subgenus]] ''[[Urosphena]]'' within the genus ''[[Cettia]]''.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Delacour |first=J. |date=2008-04-03 |title=The Bush-Warblers of the genera ''Cettia'' and ''Bradypterus'', with notes on allied genera and species |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1474-919X.1942.tb03450.x |journal=[[Ibis (journal)|Ibis]] |language=en |volume=84 |issue=4 |pages=509–519 |doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1942.tb03450.x|url-access=subscription }}</ref> The American ornithologist Ben King raised ''Urosphena'' to the status of a genus in 1989.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=King |first=Ben |date=1989 |title=The avian genera ''Tesia'' and ''Urosphena'' |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/40027037#page/178/mode/1up |journal=[[Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club]] |location=London |publisher=[[British Ornithologists' Club]] |volume=109 |pages=162–166 |via=[[Biodiversity Heritage Library]]}}</ref>

The name of the genus, ''Urosphena'', means wedge-tailed and is derived from the [[Ancient Greek]] words ''oura'' (tail) and ''sphēnos'' (wedge). The [[Specific name (zoology)|specific name]] ''whiteheadi'' refers to [[John Whitehead (explorer)|John Whitehead]], a British explorer who collected the specimens based on which this species was described.<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite book |last=Jobling |first=James A. |title=Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names |publisher=[[Christopher Helm Publishers|Christopher Helm]] |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 |location=London |pages=397, 407 |language=en |oclc=1040808348}}</ref> Bornean stubtail is the official [[common name]] designated by the [[International Ornithologists' Union]] (IOU).<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |name-list-style=amp |date=2023-07-15 |editor-last=Gill |editor-first=Frank |editor2-last=Donsker |editor2-first=David |editor3-last=Rasmussen |editor3-first=Pamela |title=Cupwings, crombecs, cettiid bush warblers, Streaked Scrub Warbler, yellow flycatchers, hylias |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/crombecs/ |access-date=2023-09-14 |website=IOC World Bird List |version=v 13.2 |language=en-US}}</ref> Other common names for the species are short-tailed bush warbler, short-tailed stubtail, and Whitehead's stubtail.<ref>{{Cite web |title=''Urosphena whiteheadi'' (Bornean Stubtail) |url=https://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?lang=EN&avibaseid=C83F6098C9145719&sec=summary |access-date=2022-03-11 |website=[[Avibase]]}}</ref>

The Bornean stubtail is classified by the IOU as one of 32 species in the cettiid warbler [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] [[Cettiidae]].<ref name=":3" /> However, some authorities classify Cettiidae as a [[subfamily]] in an expanded [[Scotocercidae]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Winkler |first1=David W. |title=Bush Warblers and Allies (Scotocercidae) |date=2020-03-04 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/scotoc1/1.0/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |editor-last=Billerman |editor-first=Shawn M. |publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.scotoc1.01 |access-date=2022-03-11 |last2=Billerman |first2=Shawn M. |last3=Lovette |first3=Irby J. |s2cid=216318390 |editor2-last=Keeney |editor2-first=Brooke K. |editor3-last=Rodewald |editor3-first=Paul G. |editor4-last=Schulenberg |editor4-first=Thomas S. |name-list-style=amp |url-access=subscription }}</ref> Within the family, it is currently placed in ''Urosphena'', a genus of three species of undistinguished brownish birds with short tails.<ref name=":3" /><ref name="phylogeny">{{Cite journal |last1=Alström |first1=Per |last2=Höhna |first2=Sebastian |last3=Gelang |first3=Magnus |last4=Ericson |first4=Per GP |last5=Olsson |first5=Urban |name-list-style=amp |date=2011 |title=Non-monophyly and intricate morphological evolution within the avian family Cettiidae revealed by multilocus analysis of a taxonomically densely sampled dataset |journal=BMC Evolutionary Biology |language=en |volume=11 |issue=1 |page=352 |doi=10.1186/1471-2148-11-352 |pmc=3261208 |pmid=22142197 |doi-access=free|bibcode=2011BMCEE..11..352A }}</ref> A 2011 study of [[Mitochondrial DNA|mitochondrial]] and [[nuclear DNA]] found that within the family, the present species is most closely related to the Asian and [[Timor stubtail]]s. These species are [[Sister group|sister]] (most closely related) to a [[clade]] (group of organisms descending from a common ancestor) formed by the [[pale-footed bush warbler]] and [[Neumann's warbler]]. The following cladogram shows relationships within the genus according to the study:{{Efn|The study did not include samples of the [[Timor stubtail]]. However, the authors hypothesised that it was most closely related to the [[Asian stubtail|Asian]] and Bornean stubtails due to similarities in appearance and vocalisations.<ref name="phylogeny" />}}<ref name="phylogeny" />

== Description ==
[[File:Urosphena whiteheadi 102414918.jpg|alt=small brownish bird with long yellowish-brown eyebrow standing on branch|left|thumb|A Bornean stubtail in [[Kinabalu Park]]]]
The Bornean stubtail is a very small warbler with long legs and a very short tail, measuring {{Convert|9.5–10|cm|in|abbr=on}} in length and having an average mass of {{Convert|10.4|g|oz|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Boyce |first1=Andy J. |last2=Mouton |first2=James C. |last3=Lloyd |first3=Penn |last4=Wolf |first4=Blair O. |last5=Martin |first5=Thomas E. |date=2020 |name-list-style=amp |title=Metabolic rate is negatively linked to adult survival but does not explain latitudinal differences in songbirds |url=http://www.andyjboyce.com/uploads/4/6/5/5/46555653/boyce_et_al._2020_ecology_letters_metabolic_rate_does_not_explain_latitudinal_variation_in_survival.pdf |journal=Ecology Letters |language=en |volume=23 |issue=4 |pages=642–652 [646] |doi=10.1111/ele.13464 |pmid=31990148 |bibcode=2020EcolL..23..642B |s2cid=210936008 |access-date=2022-03-11 |archive-date=2020-10-24 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201024145923/http://www.andyjboyce.com/uploads/4/6/5/5/46555653/boyce_et_al._2020_ecology_letters_metabolic_rate_does_not_explain_latitudinal_variation_in_survival.pdf }}</ref> The top of the head and the {{Birdgloss|upperparts}} are brown, while the {{Birdgloss|underparts}} are greyish-white, turning grey at the side of the breast and the {{Birdgloss|flanks}}. The belly is often light yellow-brown. The {{Birdgloss|supercilium}} (stripe above the eye) is long and [[Buff (color)|buffish]]-brown, with an equally long dark grey {{Birdgloss|eyestripe}} (stripe through the eye) and a thin yellow [[eye-ring]]. The cheeks and ear-coverts are orange-buff. The legs are pale pink, the bill is dark brownish-black, and the [[Iris (anatomy)|iris]] is blackish. Both sexes look similar.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Clement |first=Peter |date=2020-03-04 |editor-last=Billerman |editor-first=Shawn M. |editor2-last=Keeney |editor2-first=Brooke K. |editor3-last=Rodewald |editor3-first=Paul G. |editor4-last=Schulenberg |editor4-first=Thomas S. |name-list-style=amp |title=Bornean Stubtail (''Urosphena whiteheadi'') |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/borstu1/1.0/introduction |language=en |publisher=[[Cornell Lab of Ornithology]] |doi=10.2173/bow.borstu1.01 |url-access=subscription |access-date=2022-03-10 |journal=Birds of the World|s2cid=216190857 }}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Myers |first=Susan |url= |title=Birds of Borneo: Sabah, Sarawak, Brunei and Kalimantan |publisher=[[Christopher Helm Publishers|Christopher Helm]] |others=Illustrated by Richard Allen, Hilary Burn, Clive Byers, Daniel Cole, John Cox, Anthony Disley, Alan Harris, Szabolcs Kokay, Mike Langman, Ian Lewington, Andrew Mackay, Stephen Message, Christopher Schmidt, Jan Wilczur, and Tim Worfolk |year=2016 |isbn=978-1-4729-2444-5 |edition=Second |location=London |page=248 |language=en |oclc=944318084}}</ref>

=== Vocalisations ===
The Bornean stubtail's vocalisations are poorly known, with the species usually staying silent. Its song is a single extremely high-pitched note 0.3–0.5 seconds long. The most common sound is a barely audible, high-pitched ''tsit-tst tseee'' or ''tzi-tzi-tzeeee'' 1.4 seconds long. Another vocalisation is a slightly lower, trilled ''piririt'' around 0.7 seconds long.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" />

== Distribution and habitat ==
[[Endemism|Endemic]] to Borneo, the Bornean stubtail is found in the mountain ranges of Borneo, from Mount Kinabalu south to Liang Kubung, the [[Müller Mountains]], [[Mount Dulit]], and Gunung Menyapa. It has also been recorded from the southeastern part of the [[Meratus Mountains]] in the southeastern part of the island. The species inhabits the [[forest floor]] and [[undergrowth]] of [[montane forest]]s at altitudes of {{Convert|750–3150|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" />

== Behaviour and ecology ==
The species is usually found alone.<ref name=":0" /> It has a [[Generation time|generation length]] of 3.6 years.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" />

The Bornean stubtail feeds on [[invertebrate]]s, especially green [[aphid]]s. It forages on the ground and in dense undergrowth, creeping about in an inconspicuous manner more like that of a mouse than a bird.<ref name=":0" /> However, it can sometimes be quite curious and docile.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" />

Nests consist mostly of reddish plant fibres and are built on mossy banks.<ref name=":0" /> The [[Egg incubation|incubation]] of the eggs takes an average of 24 days, which is unusually long compared to other species in its range. The length is caused by the long periods of time (around 6–8 hours every day) that parents spend away from the nest.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Martin |first1=Thomas E. |last2=Ton |first2=Riccardo |last3=Oteyza |first3=Juan C. |name-list-style=amp |date=2018 |title=Adaptive influence of extrinsic and intrinsic factors on variation of incubation periods among tropical and temperate passerines |journal=[[The Auk]] |language=en |volume=135 |issue=1 |pages=101–113 |doi=10.1642/AUK-17-124.1 |s2cid=26470996 |doi-access=free |bibcode=2018Auk...135..101M }}</ref>

== Status ==
The Bornean stubtail is classified as being of [[Least-concern species|least concern]] by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]] due to its sufficiently large range and stable population. Its population has not been estimated, but it is a common species above elevations of {{Convert|2000|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /> It is present in some protected areas like [[Kinabalu Park]].<ref name=":0" />
